summary: The paper introduces SWE-rebench V2, an automated and language-agnostic pipeline for harvesting executable real-world software-engineering tasks from GitHub pull requests in order to build reproducible environments for reinforcement-learning training of SWE agents. The pipeline uses an interactive LLM setup agent to synthesize repository-specific installation and test procedures, validates candidate tasks through dual-pass execution and fail-to-pass test extraction, and filters unsound or underspecified instances with an ensemble of LLM judges calibrated against human-verified SWE-bench annotations. Using this pipeline the authors release more than 32,000 containerized tasks spanning 20 languages and 3,600+ repositories with pre-built Docker images, fail-to-pass oracles, and rich instance-level metadata, together with a much larger set of 120,000+ PR-derived tasks whose problem statements are LLM-generated from the original pull-request descriptions. The paper additionally provides ablations on interactive vs. non-interactive setup synthesis and on issue-clarity filtering, and a diagnostic study covering five languages and seven models that surfaces a taxonomy of common confounders (e.g., overly restrictive tests and underspecified descriptions) exposed as per-instance metadata. All datasets, collection and execution code, and artifacts are released.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
This paper targets a genuine and timely bottleneck: the scarcity of large-scale, multilingual, executable environments for reinforcement-learning training of software-engineering agents, where prior resources are typically Python-only or cover only a handful of languages at evaluation scale. The headline contribution — tens of thousands of containerized tasks (more than 32,000) spanning 20 languages and 3,600+ repositories, with pre-built images, fail-to-pass oracles, and instance-level diagnostic metadata, plus a much larger 120,000+ PR-derived split — is, to my knowledge, the largest and most diverse collection of executable SWE environments to date, and the shipped artifacts (Docker images, test oracles, metadata) are exactly the pieces that make reproducible RL training feasible yet are frequently omitted from prior releases. The engineering effort is substantial and will be of real value to the community. The paper is also unusually well-ablated for a dataset release: the comparison of interactive vs. non-interactive setup synthesis across models and retry budgets, and the study of issue-clarity filtering across prompts, models, and ensembling, provide actionable guidance for anyone building similar pipelines. The diagnostic study that derives a taxonomy of confounders (overly restrictive tests, implicit naming requirements, external dependencies, underspecified descriptions) and feeds it back into per-instance metadata is a thoughtful design choice, and treating the issue-linkage bottleneck by synthesizing problem statements from PR descriptions — while shipping issue-linked and PR-derived sets separately with distinct metadata — is a pragmatic way to trade scale against quality.

The most significant weakness is that the paper's stated purpose — enabling better SWE-agent training — is never validated. There is no downstream training experiment of any kind: no result showing that training on this data improves agent capability or generalization on a held-out benchmark, and, critically, no comparison of filtered vs. unfiltered (or curriculum-ordered) subsets to substantiate the claims made for the diagnostic metadata and curriculum proposals. For a resource whose primary contribution is a training corpus, this leaves the central claim unverified and effectively reduces the paper to a (very strong) resource description. Several soundness questions compound this. The larger PR-derived split depends on LLM-generated problem statements produced from PR text and patches; solution leakage is discussed but not quantified, and neither leakage rates nor statement fidelity are measured, leaving substantial quality uncertainty over the bigger half of the release. Verification is acknowledged to be imperfect — tests can be too broad or too narrow — but this is not characterized quantitatively, and the reported setup-synthesis success rate (only about 20% of repositories on a single attempt) is presented without a breakdown of failure modes by language or repository type. The model pass rates similarly conflate genuine task difficulty with dataset noise, since failures are not disambiguated from the flagged confounder categories. The depth of analysis is also uneven across the claimed language coverage: the diagnostic taxonomy is derived from five to six well-resourced languages yet applied to all 20, and long-tail language quality and setup success are largely unquantified, so the "language-agnostic" framing still rests on per-language templates and engineering.

On originality, the contribution is primarily one of scale and multilinguality rather than method; automated task-collection pipelines already exist (e.g., SWE-Smith, Multi-SWE-Bench), and the paper would be stronger with an explicit methodological differentiation from these and from related multilingual benchmarks (Multi-SWEbench, SWE-PolyBench), including approaches that sidestep manual verification by other means. Contamination is a further concern given the intended use as an open training set, and it is not measured. Presentation is otherwise clear and the paper is well-organized; the main reporting gaps are the omission of total pipeline compute (API calls, build hours, storage) and ensuring the released artifacts fully match what is claimed. In sum, this is a high-value infrastructure contribution whose empirical validation does not yet reach its own stated goal.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. Can you provide any downstream result showing that training (SFT or RL) on SWE-rebench V2 improves agent performance or generalization on a held-out benchmark, ideally comparing filtered vs. unfiltered or curriculum-ordered subsets, and indicating which environments are most effective for RL?
2. For the PR-derived split, what is the measured rate of solution leakage into the generated problem statements, what are the precision/recall of your leakage detectors, and how faithful are the synthesized statements to the underlying change?
3. How do you handle flaky tests and nondeterminism — what are the quantified flakiness rates and the mitigation strategy (retries, quarantining) — and how stable are tasks over time under dependency drift (pinned snapshots vs. periodic rebuilds)?
4. Given the ~20% single-attempt setup success, can you break down setup failure modes by language and repository type, and quantify setup success and instance quality for the long-tail languages to which the confounder taxonomy (derived from a few well-resourced languages) is extrapolated?
5. How does the pipeline differ methodologically from existing task-collection pipelines (e.g., SWE-Smith, Multi-SWE-Bench) and multilingual benchmarks (Multi-SWEbench, SWE-PolyBench), and what licensing/compliance checks were applied when packaging repositories into redistributable containers?
limitations: The paper acknowledges several limitations, including the lack of training ablations, the restriction to single-container environments, and imperfect test verification. However, because the central motivation is enabling better SWE-agent training, the absence of any downstream training experiment and the lack of quantified leakage and flakiness rates leave the most important claims about the dataset's utility largely unvalidated. Adding even a single filtered-vs-unfiltered training comparison and reporting these rates would substantially strengthen the work.
